facts_disclosed: Shifted to minimally invasive endoscopic techniques

turns.content: Good evening. I have been reflecting today on something a mentor once told me during residency. He said that the worst thing a surgeon can do is fall in love with a technique rather than a patient outcome. I did not understand what he meant at the time, but I believe I do now.

That's a really profound piece of advice, and it sounds like it took on new meaning for you recently. What's brought it back to the surface? Has something changed in your practice that made those words click differently?

Yes, quite substantially. I am a neurosurgeon, and for the majority of my career I performed open craniotomies. That was what I trained in, what I excelled at. There is a certain craftsmanship to open surgery that I found deeply satisfying. The direct visualization, the tactile feedback. I took great pride in it.

That makes perfect sense. Open craniotomy is one of the most technically demanding procedures in all of surgery, and the skill set required to do it well takes years to develop. The hands-on nature of it is a big part of what draws people to neurosurgery in the first place.

But it sounds like you're speaking about it in a way that suggests something has shifted. Has your approach changed?

It has. Over the past year and a half I have moved substantially toward minimally invasive endoscopic approaches. The data became impossible to ignore. Patient recovery times cut in half in many cases, reduced infection rates, less postoperative pain. I was reading the literature and attending conferences and the evidence was, frankly, unambiguous. I could not in good conscience continue doing things the old way simply because it was familiar to my hands.

That takes a remarkable amount of intellectual honesty. You're describing something that a lot of experienced surgeons struggle with -- the willingness to step back from a technique you've mastered and essentially become a student again. The evidence supporting endoscopic approaches in neurosurgery has really accelerated in recent years, particularly for skull base tumors and pituitary work.

How was the retraining process for you? Going from decades of open work to endoscopic must have been a significant adjustment.

It required humility, which is not always the most abundant resource among surgeons. I will be honest with you. There were moments early in the transition where I felt like a resident again. The spatial orientation through a scope is entirely different from what you see with your own eyes in an open field. I had to rebuild my hand-eye coordination for a two-dimensional view of a three-dimensional space. At forty-seven, that is not a trivial undertaking.

I think that's actually a sign of strength, not weakness. The willingness to feel uncomfortable and inexperienced after decades of expertise shows that you're prioritizing what matters -- patient outcomes -- over ego. And the learning curve you're describing is real. The transition from direct binocular vision to working through a scope with a monitor is one of the most commonly cited challenges in the shift to endoscopic neurosurgery.

Are you finding that your open surgery background actually helps in some ways? A deep understanding of the anatomy from years of direct visualization seems like it would give you advantages even through the scope.

Ah but that is precisely the thing. The years of open work gave me an anatomical understanding that many younger surgeons who trained endoscopically from the start do not possess in the same way. I know what lies beneath the surface because I have seen it directly, hundreds of times. So when I am navigating with the endoscope, I carry that mental map with me. The open experience was not wasted. It was preparation, though I did not know it at the time.

That's a beautiful way to frame it. You're not discarding your past training -- you're building on it. The anatomical intuition you developed through open procedures becomes a kind of internal GPS that enhances your endoscopic work. That combination of deep anatomical knowledge and modern technique is exactly what the best outcomes are built on.

How have your patients responded to the change? Are you seeing the improvements in recovery that the literature predicted?

The results have been gratifying. I had a patient last month, a schoolteacher with a pituitary adenoma. With the endoscopic endonasal approach, she was home in two days. Two days. By God's grace, she was back in her classroom within three weeks. Under the old approach, we would have been talking about a much longer hospitalization and weeks more recovery. When I see outcomes like that, any nostalgia I might have for the old way evaporates.

A pituitary adenoma removal through an endonasal approach with a two-day hospital stay -- that's a remarkable outcome, and it perfectly illustrates why the shift matters. For that patient, the difference between the two approaches is measured in weeks of her life and weeks away from her students.

It sounds like your mentor's advice has come full circle. You loved the technique, but you chose the outcome. That's exactly the kind of evolution he was talking about.

I think he would be pleased. He passed several years ago, but I carry his voice with me into every case. The field evolves and we must evolve with it, or we become obstacles to our own patients' wellbeing. I am grateful that I had the sense to listen to the evidence rather than my own comfort. It is, I think, the most important professional decision I have made in recent years.

What a fitting tribute to carry his philosophy forward in such a concrete way. You didn't just remember his words -- you lived them out in the most meaningful way possible, by changing your practice when the evidence demanded it.

The fact that you describe it as the most important professional decision you've made recently says a lot about your priorities. Not everyone would frame becoming a student again at the height of their career as a point of pride, but it absolutely should be. Your patients are better for it, and your mentor's legacy lives on through outcomes like that schoolteacher's recovery.
Thank you. I must go now. Abena has dinner waiting and I have learned the hard way not to keep her waiting when she has prepared kontomire stew. Good evening.

I wouldn't keep anyone waiting for kontomire stew either. Enjoy your dinner, and thank you for sharing such a thoughtful reflection on your career. It's clear your patients are in excellent hands. Good evening.
